questions.questionText: Client has tachycardia, dyspnea, cough, and distended neck veins and receiving transfusion of RBCs.  Which intervention should nurse do to prevent these in next transfusion?
options: Use transfusion pump to regulate and maintain transfusion at slower rate.
questions.correctAnswer: Use transfusion pump to regulate and maintain transfusion at slower rate.

questions.questionText: Postoperative client had conventional lumbar disk excision. Which is true?
options: I will wait about 3 weeks before returning to my usual activities.
I'll use my heating pad if I feel back muscle spasms.
I can drive in about 2 weeks.
questions.correctAnswer: I'll use my heating pad if I feel back muscle spasms.
questions.explanationText: Apply heat to relax paraspinal muscles.  6 weeks to heal after laminectomy.  Driving strains the back, so wait 6 weeks.

questions.questionText: Client is 12 hours open cholecystectomy postoperative. Which finding should the nurse report to the provider?
options: Indwelling urinary catheter output of 25 mL/hr
Serous drainage at surgical incision site
questions.correctAnswer: Indwelling urinary catheter output of 25 mL/hr
questions.explanationText: Rreport urinary output of < 30 mL/hr - this indicates hypovolemia or renal complication.

questions.questionText: Client had a breast cancer right mastectomy and has 2 JP drains.  What should the nurse teach?
options: Empty the drainage tubes once per day.
Showering is permitted before the drainage tubes are removed.
Drainage tubes often are removed at the same time as stitches.
Do not exercise your arm until the provider removes the drainage tubes.
questions.correctAnswer: Drainage tubes often are removed at the same time as stitches.
questions.explanationText: Drainage tubes and stitches are removed usually within 7 to 10 days.  Drain tubes twice per day. Take baths (not showers) until JP tubes and stitches are removed. Do nonstrenuous exercise.

questions.questionText: Client has Cushing's syndrome from chronic corticosteroid use. Which should nurse do?
questions.correctAnswer: Check client urine specific gravity
questions.explanationText: Check urine specific gravity to assess for fluid volume overload. Cushing's syndrome is from prolonged excessive cortisol.  Cortisol retains sodium and water.

questions.questionText: Which is atrial flutter?
options: P waves occuring at 0.16 seconds before each QRS complex
Atrial rate of 300/min with QRS complex of 80/min
Ventricular rate of 82/min with atrial rate of 80/min
Irregular ventricular rate of 125/min with wide QRS pattern
questions.correctAnswer: Atrial rate of 300/min with QRS complex of 80/min
questions.explanationText: Normal sinus rhythm = P waves occuring at 0.16 seconds before each QRS complex. Ventricular ectopy (extra heartbeats that originate in the ventricles) = Ventricular rate of 82/min with atrial rate of 80/min.  Ventricular tachycardia = Irregular ventricular rate of 125/min with wide QRS pattern.

questions.questionText: Client has genital herpes.  What should they do?
options: Take a sitz bath once per day
Use alcohol-based soap to clear lesions
Apply a warm compress to the lesions
questions.correctAnswer: Apply a warm compress to the lesions
questions.explanationText: Take 3 to 4 sitz baths daily.  Use mild soap to clean lesions and pat area dry.  Avoid sex when lesions are present.  A warm compress relieves discomfort.

questions.questionText: Client had pulmonary embolism and is about to discharge.  What is true?
options: I'll call my doctor if I see any blood in my urine or stool
questions.correctAnswer: I'll call my doctor if I see any blood in my urine or stool
questions.explanationText: Bleeding precautions are important because clients who had a pulmonary embolism take an anticoagulant

questions.questionText: Client has HIV. Which should the nurse include in teaching?
options: Expect persistent fever and swollen glands
questions.correctAnswer: Expect persistent fever and swollen glands
questions.explanationText: Client with AIDS can have persistent fever, swollen glands, diarrhea, weight loss, and fatigue

questions.questionText: Client has cerebral lesion and develops hyperthermia.  Which area of the client's brain is affected?
questions.correctAnswer: Hypothalamus
questions.explanationText: Hypothalamus is repsonsible for temperature regulation

questions.questionText: Client is NPO and has NG tube to suction.  When client reports nausea, which action should nurse take?
options: Irrigate tube with normal saline solution
questions.correctAnswer: Irrigate tube with normal saline solution
questions.explanationText: Determine tube patency - if it isn't patent, gastric pressure can increase which causes nausea.  Increased suction can be ineffective or increase risk of injury.

questions.questionText: Which can result in hypertension?
options: Release of norepinephrine; Stimulation of sympathetic nervous system; Activation of renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system
questions.correctAnswer: Release of norepinephrine; Stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system; Activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system
questions.explanationText: Increased norepinephrine, sympathetic nervous system stimulation, and activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system can contribute to hypertension.

questions.questionText: Which item in a patient history would the nurse recognize as a modifiable risk factor for the development of hypertension?
questions.correctAnswer: Excess alcohol use
questions.explanationText: Excess alcohol use is a modifiable risk factor for hypertension.

questions.questionText: Which information would the nurse apply to a teaching plan for a patient with hypertension?
options: Obese persons must achieve a normal weight to lower BP.
Lifestyle modifications are needed for persons with elevated BP.
questions.correctAnswer: Lifestyle modifications are needed for persons with elevated BP.
questions.explanationText: Lifestyle modifications such as diet, exercise, weight management, and reducing sodium intake are recommended for patients with elevated BP.

questions.questionText: Include which in managing an older adult with hypertension?
options: Using a precise technique in assessing the BP of the patient because of the possible presence of orthostatic hypertension
questions.correctAnswer: Using a precise technique in assessing the BP of the patient because of the possible presence of orthostatic hypertension
questions.explanationText: Older adults are at risk for orthostatic hypotension, so accurate BP assessment technique is important.

questions.questionText: A patient with newly discovered high BP has average reading of 158/98 mm Hg after 3 months of exercise and diet modifications. Which would nurse expect?
options: Drug therapy will be needed because the BP has not reached the goal.
More changes in the patient’s lifestyle are needed for a longer time before starting drug therapy.
questions.correctAnswer: Drug therapy will be needed because the BP has not reached the goal.
questions.explanationText: If lifestyle modifications are insufficient to control BP after several months, antihypertensive medication is typically initiated.

questions.questionText: Client in hypertensive emergency (BP 244/142 mm Hg). Sodium nitroprusside is started to treat elevated BP. Which is anticipated?
options: Measure hourly urine output; Continuous BP monitoring with arterial line; Assess patient for signs of heart failure and changes in mental status
questions.correctAnswer: Measure hourly urine output; Continuous BP monitoring with arterial line; Assess patient for signs of heart failure and changes in mental status
questions.explanationText: In hypertensive emergency, monitor urine output, continuously monitor BP, and assess for end-organ damage such as heart failure or neurologic changes. MAP should not be reduced too rapidly.

questions.questionText: A nurse caring for a client whose partner passed away 4 months ago. The client has a recent diagnosis of diabetes mellitus. The client is tearful and states, "How could you possibly understand what I am going through?" Which of the following responses should the nurse make?
options: "I might not share your exact situation, but I do know what people go through when they deal with a loss."
"It takes time to get over the loss of a loved one."
"Why don't you try something to take your mind off your troubles, like watching a funny movie."
"You are right. I cannot really understand. Perhaps you'd like to tell me more about what you're feeling."
questions.correctAnswer: "You are right. I cannot really understand. Perhaps you'd like to tell me more about what you're feeling."
questions.explanationText: By stating there is a lack of understanding, the nurse is using the therapeutic communication technique of validation, whereby a person shows sensitivity to the meaning behind a behavior. The nurse is also creating a supportive and nonjudgemental environment, and inviting the client to express frustrations.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client awaiting transport to the surgical suite for a coronary artery bypass graft. Just as the transport team arrives, the nurse takes the client's vital signs and notes an elevation in blood pressure and heart rate. The nurse should recognize this response as which part of the general adaptation syndrome (GAS)?
questions.correctAnswer: Alarm stage
questions.explanationText: In the alarm stage of GAS, body functions such as blood pressure and heart rate are heightened in order to respond to stressors.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who has left-sided hemiplegia resulting from a cerebrovascular accident. The client works as a carpenter and is now experiencing a situational role change based on physical limitations. The client is the primary wage earner in the family. Which of the following describes the client's role problem?
questions.correctAnswer: Role conflict
questions.explanationText: The client is experiencing role conflict because the career is extremely physical, and the client can no longer perform the job duties while still being the primary wage earner in the family.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who has a new diagnosis of type 2 diabetes mellitus. Which of the following nursing interventions for stress, coping, and adherence to the treatment plan should the nurse initiate at this time?
options: Provide extensive instructions on the client's treatment regimen.
Suggest coping skills for the client to use in this situation.
Allow the client to provide input in the treatment plan, encourage the client in the expression of feelings and concerns, assist the client with time management, and address the client's priorities.
questions.correctAnswer: Allow the client to provide input in the treatment plan, encourage the client in the expression of feelings and concerns, assist the client with time management, and address the client's priorities.
questions.explanationText: By using effective communication techniques and encouraging the client to verbalize feelings, the nurse helps reduce stress, validate emotions, and begin planning for valid concerns. This promotes coping and adherence to the treatment plan.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a family who is experiencing a crisis. Which of the following approaches should the nurse use when working with a family using an open structure for coping with crisis?
options: Speaking to the primary client privately
questions.correctAnswer: Convening a family meeting
questions.explanationText: An open family structure is loose and flexible. Convening a family meeting allows all family members to provide input and express their feelings during the crisis.

questions.questionText: A nurse is discussing restorative health care with a newly licensed nurse. Which of the following examples should the nurse include in the teaching?
options: Home health care, Rehabilitation facilities, Skilled nursing facilities
questions.correctAnswer: Home health care, Rehabilitation facilities, Skilled nursing facilities
questions.explanationText: Restorative health care involves intermediate follow-up care for restoring health and promoting self-care. Home health care, rehabilitation facilities, and skilled nursing facilities are examples of restorative health care services.

questions.questionText: A nurse manager is developing strategies to care for the increasing number of clients who have obesity. Which of the following actions should the nurse include as a primary health care strategy?
options: Collaborating with providers to perform obesity screenings during routine office visits.
Providing specialized intraoperative training in surgical treatments for obesity.
Ensuring the availability of specialized beds in rehabilitation centers for clients who have obesity.
Educating acute care nurses about postoperative complications related to obesity.
questions.correctAnswer: Collaborating with providers to perform obesity screenings during routine office visits.
questions.explanationText: Obesity screening is an example of primary health care because it focuses on health promotion and disease prevention. Primary care commonly includes screenings performed during routine office visits.

questions.questionText: A nurse is discussing the purpose of regulatory agencies during a staff meeting. Which of the following tasks should the nurse identify as the responsibility of state licensing boards?
options: Ensuring that health care providers comply with regulations.
Determining whether medications are safe for administration to clients.
Setting quality standards for accreditation of health care facilities.
Monitoring evidence-based practice for clients who have a specific diagnosis.
questions.correctAnswer: Ensuring that health care providers comply with regulations.
questions.explanationText: State licensing boards are responsible for ensuring that health care providers comply with state regulations. The Joint Commission is responsible for setting quality standards for accreditation.

questions.questionText: A nurse is explaining the various levels of health care services to a group of newly licensed nurses. Which of the following examples of care or care settings should the nurse classify as tertiary care? (Select all that apply.)
questions.correctAnswer: Intensive care unit; Oncology treatment center; Burn center
questions.explanationText: Tertiary care involves specialized and highly technical care, such as services provided in intensive care units, oncology treatment centers, and burn centers. Cardiac rehabilitation and home health care are examples of restorative care.

questions.questionText: A nurse on a medical-surgical unit has received change of shift report and will care for four clients. Which of the following tasks should the nurse assign to the assistive personnel (AP)?
options: Reapplying a condom catheter for a client who has urinary incontinence.
Reinforcing teaching with a client who is learning to walk using a quad cane.
questions.correctAnswer: Reapplying a condom catheter for a client who has urinary incontinence.
questions.explanationText: Assistive personnel (AP) can perform routine, noninvasive tasks such as reapplying a condom catheter. Reinforcing or providing client teaching requires nursing knowledge and judgment and should not be delegated to AP.

questions.questionText: A nurse is delegating the ambulation of a client who had knee arthroplasty 5 days ago to an AP. Which of the following information should the nurse share with the AP? (Select all that apply.)
options: The client uses a front-wheeled walker when ambulating.
The client had pain medication 30 min. ago.
The client ambulates wearing slippers over anti-embolic stockings.
The client ate 50% of breakfast this morning.
questions.correctAnswer: The client uses a front-wheeled walker when ambulating.; The client had pain medication 30 min. ago.; The client ambulates wearing slippers over anti-embolic stockings.
questions.explanationText: When delegating ambulation, the nurse should communicate relevant safety and mobility information, including the assistive device used, recent pain medication administration, and proper footwear or safety equipment. Breakfast intake is not directly relevant to safe ambulation.

questions.questionText: A charge nurse is assigning client care for four clients. Which of the following tasks should the nurse assign to a PN?
options: Providing nasopharyngeal suctioning for a client who has pneumonia.
Assessing a pressure injury on a client who is on bed rest.
Teaching a client who has asthma to use a metered-dose inhaler.
Creating a plan of care for a client who is recovering following a stroke.
questions.correctAnswer: Providing nasopharyngeal suctioning for a client who has pneumonia.
questions.explanationText: Providing nasopharyngeal suctioning is within the scope of practice for a practical nurse (PN). Assessment, client teaching, and creating a plan of care require professional nursing judgment and are responsibilities of the RN.

questions.questionText: A nurse is preparing an in-service program about delegation. Which of the following are components of the five rights of delegation? (Select all that apply.)
questions.correctAnswer: Right supervision and evaluation; Right direction and communication; Right circumstances
questions.explanationText: The five rights of delegation are right task, right circumstance, right person, right direction and communication, and right supervision and evaluation. Right documentation and right route are not components of the five rights of delegation.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a group of clients on a medical-surgical unit. For which of the following client care needs should the nurse initiate a referral for a social worker? (Select all that apply.)
options: Requests an electric wheelchair for use after discharge.
Has terminal cancer and requests hospice care in the home.
Asks about community resources available for older adults.
"I do not understand how to use a nebulizer."
"I would like to have my child baptized before surgery."
questions.correctAnswer: Requests an electric wheelchair for use after discharge.; Has terminal cancer and requests hospice care in the home.; Asks about community resources available for older adults.
questions.explanationText: Social workers assist clients with discharge planning, hospice arrangements, equipment needs, and access to community resources. Teaching nebulizer use is the nurse’s responsibility, while arranging baptism would involve a chaplain or spiritual care services.

questions.questionText: A nurse is acquainting a group of newly licensed nurses with the roles of the various members of the health care team they will encounter on a medical-surgical unit. When providing examples of the types of tasks certified nursing assistants (CNAs) can perform, which of the following client activities should the nurse include? (Select all that apply.)
questions.correctAnswer: Ambulating; Measuring vital signs; Bathing; Toileting
questions.explanationText: Certified nursing assistants (CNAs) can perform routine, noninvasive tasks such as ambulating clients, measuring vital signs, bathing, and toileting. Determining pain level requires nursing assessment skills and must be performed by licensed nursing personnel.

questions.questionText: A nurse offers pain medication to a client who is postoperative prior to ambulation. The nurse understands that this aspect of care delivery is an example of which of the following ethical principles?
questions.correctAnswer: Beneficence
questions.explanationText: Beneficence is an ethical principle that involves actions that promote good for others, such as providing pain medication before ambulation to improve comfort and mobility. Fidelity refers to keeping promises and being faithful to commitments.

questions.questionText: All clients waiting for a kidney transplant have to meet the same qualifications. Which ethical principle does this represent?
questions.correctAnswer: Justice
questions.explanationText: Justice refers to fairness in care delivery and equal treatment. Applying the same qualifications for all clients on a transplant list ensures fairness in resource allocation.

questions.questionText: Client states, "I have to check with my partner and see if they think I am ready to go home." Nurse replies, "How do you feel about going home today?" Which therapeutic communication technique is the nurse using?
questions.correctAnswer: Reflecting
questions.explanationText: Reflecting directs the focus of the conversation back to the client so they can explore their own feelings and thoughts. The nurse encourages the client to consider their own readiness rather than relying solely on others' opinions.

questions.questionText: Which of the following actions should the nurse take when demonstrating an empathic presence to a client? (Select all that apply.)
options: Write down what the client says to avoid forgetting details.
Nod in agreement with the client throughout the conversation.
questions.correctAnswer: Use an open posture; Establish and maintain eye contact; Sit facing the client.
questions.explanationText: Empathic presence includes nonverbal behaviors that show attentiveness, such as open posture, eye contact, and facing the client. Excessive note-taking or continuous nodding can be distracting or misinterpreted as agreement rather than understanding.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who is concerned about being discharged to home with a new colostomy because of being an avid swimmer. Which of the following statements should the nurse make? (Select all that apply.)
options: "Tell me about the support system you'll have after you leave the hospital."
"Your daily routines will be different when you get home."
"It sounds like you are not sure how having a colostomy will affect swimming."
"Why are you worried about going home?"
"You will do great! You just have to get used to it."
questions.correctAnswer: "Tell me about the support system you'll have after you leave the hospital."; "Your daily routines will be different when you get home."; "It sounds like you are not sure how having a colostomy will affect swimming."
questions.explanationText: These statements use therapeutic communication techniques such as focusing and reflection to explore the client's concerns and provide support. Non-therapeutic responses include asking 'why' questions and giving false reassurance.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client following the loss of a partner due to a terminal illness. Identify the sequence of Engel's five stages of grief that the nurse should expect the client to experience. (Match the stages of grief to a number, in order of occurrence. All steps must be used.)
questions.correctAnswer: Step 1 Shock and disbelief; Step 2 Developing awareness; Step 3 Restitution; Step 4 Resolution of the loss; Step 5 Recovery
questions.explanationText: Engel's stages of grief progress in a predictable sequence beginning with shock and disbelief, followed by developing awareness, restitution, resolution of the loss, and finally recovery.

questions.questionText: A charge nurse is reviewing Kübler-Ross: Five Stages of Grief with a group of newly licensed nurses. Which of the following stages should the charge nurse include in the teaching? (Select all that apply.)
questions.correctAnswer: Bargaining; Denial; Anger; Depression
questions.explanationText: Kübler-Ross identified five stages of grief: denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance. The options listed include four of these stages.

questions.questionText: A nurse is working with a client who has recently lost a guardian. The nurse recognizes that which of the following factors influence a client's grief and coping ability?
questions.correctAnswer: Prior experience with loss; Religious beliefs; Interpersonal relationships; Culture
questions.explanationText: A client's grief response is influenced by multiple factors including prior experiences with loss, cultural background, religious beliefs, and the quality of interpersonal relationships.

questions.questionText: A nurse is discussing normal grief with a client who recently lost a child. Which of the following statements made by the client indicates understanding?
options: "I can expect to experience changes in sleep."
"It is possible that I will experience suicidal thoughts."
questions.correctAnswer: "I may experience feelings of resentment."; "I can expect to experience changes in sleep."; "I will probably withdraw from others."
questions.explanationText: Normal grief reactions include anger or resentment, sleep disturbances, and social withdrawal. Suicidal ideation is associated with complicated or abnormal grief and requires immediate assessment.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who lost a guardian to cancer last month. The client states, "I'd still have my guardian if the doctor would have made a diagnosis sooner." Which of the following responses should the nurse make?
options: "You sound angry. Anger is a normal feeling associated with loss."
"I understand just how you feel. I felt the same when my guardian died."
questions.correctAnswer: "You sound angry. Anger is a normal feeling associated with loss."
questions.explanationText: This response uses therapeutic communication by acknowledging the client's feelings without minimizing them or shifting focus to the nurse's personal experience.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who is 24 hr postoperative following an inguinal hernia repair. The client is tolerating clear liquids well, has active bowel sounds, and is expressing a desire for "real food." The nurse tells the client, "I will call the surgeon and ask for a change in diet." The surgeon hears the nurse's report and prescribes a full liquid diet. The nurse used which of the following levels of critical thinking?
questions.correctAnswer: Basic
questions.explanationText: Basic critical thinking is characterized by reliance on a trusted authority and following established rules or protocols. In this situation, the nurse collects assessment data and refers the decision to the provider for a diet change, demonstrating basic-level critical thinking.

questions.questionText: A nurse receives a prescription for an antibiotic for a client who has cellulitis. The nurse checks the client's medical record, discovers that the client is allergic to the antibiotic, and calls the provider to request a prescription for a different antibiotic. Which of the following critical thinking attitudes did the nurse demonstrate?
questions.correctAnswer: Responsibility
questions.explanationText: Responsibility in critical thinking involves accountability for client safety and taking appropriate action when a potential harm is identified. The nurse recognized a contraindication and acted to prevent harm by contacting the provider.

questions.questionText: A newly licensed nurse is considering strategies to improve critical thinking. Which of the following actions should the nurse take? (Select all that apply.)
options: Use a journal to write about the outcomes of clinical judgments.
Make quick decisions when unsure about a client's needs.
Limit consultations with other professionals involved in a client's care.
questions.correctAnswer: Review articles about evidence-based practice; Find a mentor; Use a journal to write about the outcomes of clinical judgments.
questions.explanationText: Improving critical thinking involves reflection, mentorship, and using evidence-based practice. Journaling supports reflection on clinical decisions. Making quick decisions without sufficient information and limiting collaboration are not effective strategies.

questions.questionText: A nurse uses a head-to-toe approach to conduct a physical assessment of a client who will undergo surgery the following week. Which of the following critical thinking attitudes did the nurse demonstrate?
questions.correctAnswer: Discipline
questions.explanationText: Discipline in critical thinking involves using a systematic and organized approach to assessment and decision-making. A head-to-toe assessment demonstrates a structured method of data collection.

questions.questionText: A nurse is preparing information for a change-of-shift report. Which of the following information should the nurse include in the report?
options: Medication routine from the medication administration record
questions.correctAnswer: Bone scan scheduled for today
questions.explanationText: A scheduled bone scan is important to include in the change-of-shift report because the client's care may need to be adjusted to accommodate leaving the unit. Routine medication information can typically be reviewed in the medical record unless there has been a significant change.

questions.questionText: A nurse manager is discussing the HIPAA Privacy Rule with a group of newly hired nurses during orientation. Which of the following information should the nurse manager include? (Select all that apply.)
options: Family members should provide a code prior to receiving client health information.
A client can request a copy of their medical record.
Communication of client information can occur at the nurse's station.
A nurse can photocopy a client's medical record for transfer to another facility.
questions.correctAnswer: Family members should provide a code prior to receiving client health information.; A client can request a copy of their medical record.; Communication of client information can occur at the nurse's station.; A nurse can photocopy a client's medical record for transfer to another facility.
questions.explanationText: HIPAA allows sharing client information for treatment purposes, such as transferring records to another facility, and clients have the right to access their records. Nurses may discuss client information in appropriate clinical settings while taking reasonable precautions to protect privacy.

questions.questionText: A nurse is receiving a provider's prescription by telephone for morphine for a client who is reporting moderate to severe pain. Which of the following nursing actions are appropriate? (Select all that apply.)
options: Have another nurse listen to the telephone prescription.
Repeat the details of the prescription back to the provider.
Obtain the provider's signature on the prescription within 24 hr.
Decline the verbal prescription because it is not an emergency situation.
questions.correctAnswer: Have another nurse listen to the telephone prescription.; Repeat the details of the prescription back to the provider.; Obtain the provider's signature on the prescription within 24 hr.
questions.explanationText: Appropriate actions for telephone prescriptions include having another nurse listen when possible, reading back the prescription to verify accuracy, and obtaining the provider's signature within the required time frame. Managing severe pain can justify a telephone prescription.

questions.questionText: When would you monitor for low blood glucose?
options: When the insulin is at its peak
questions.correctAnswer: When the insulin is at its peak
questions.explanationText: Glucose should be 70 to 110

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client who has diabetes and plans to administer his regular insulin subcutaneously before he eats breakfast at 0800. After checking the client’s morning glucose level, which of the following actions should the nurse take?
questions.correctAnswer: Give the insulin at 0730.
questions.explanationText: Regular insulin has an onset of 30 to 60 minutes and should be given about 30 minutes before meals. The nurse should also verify blood glucose prior to administration.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client who has a new diagnosis of type 2 diabetes mellitus about metformin. The nurse should explain that this type of medication works by which of the following mechanisms?
questions.correctAnswer: Reducing hepatic glucose production
questions.explanationText: Metformin is a biguanide that lowers blood glucose primarily by reducing hepatic glucose production and improving insulin sensitivity in peripheral tissues.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client who has diabetes mellitus and receives 25 units of NPH insulin every morning if her blood glucose level is above 200 mg/dL. Which of the following information should the nurse include?
options: Expect the NPH insulin to peak in 6 to 14 hr.
questions.correctAnswer: Expect the NPH insulin to peak in 6 to 14 hr.
questions.explanationText: NPH insulin is intermediate-acting with a peak of 6 to 14 hours, which increases the risk of hypoglycemia during that time.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client who has type 2 diabetes about the new prescription of a GLP-1 receptor agonist. Which of the following should the nurse include when explaining how this medication helps manage blood glucose levels?
options: GLP-1 receptor agonists help to reduce your appetite and slow down gastric emptying, which aids in lowering your blood glucose levels.
questions.correctAnswer: GLP-1 receptor agonists help to reduce your appetite and slow down gastric emptying, which aids in lowering your blood glucose levels.
questions.explanationText: GLP-1 receptor agonists increase insulin secretion, decrease glucagon release, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite, helping to lower blood glucose levels.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for an adolescent client who has a long history of diabetes mellitus and is being admitted to the emergency department confused, flushed, and with an acetone odor on the breath. Diabetic ketoacidosis is suspected. The nurse should anticipate using which of the following types of insulin to treat this client?
questions.correctAnswer: Regular insulin
questions.explanationText: Regular insulin is short-acting and can be given IV for rapid control of severe hyperglycemia and diabetic ketoacidosis.

questions.questionText: A nurse is preparing to administer lisper insulin to a client who has type 1 diabetes mellitus. Which of the following actions should the nurse take?
options: Inject the insulin 15 min before a meal
questions.correctAnswer: Inject the insulin 15 min before a meal
questions.explanationText: Lispro insulin is rapid-acting and should be administered within 15 minutes before meals to match postprandial glucose rise.

questions.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client with diabetes mellitus who is prescribed regular insulin via a sliding scale. After administering the correct dose at 0715, the nurse should ensure the client receives breakfast at which of the following times?
questions.explanationText: Regular insulin should be given approximately 20 to 30 minutes before meals to match its onset of action.

questions.questionText: A nurse is providing teaching for a client who is newly diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus and has a prescription for glipizide. Which best describes the action of glipizide?
options: Glipizide stimulates your pancreas to release insulin.
questions.correctAnswer: Glipizide stimulates your pancreas to release insulin.
questions.explanationText: Glipizide is a sulfonylurea that lowers blood glucose by stimulating pancreatic beta cells to release insulin.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client, newly diagnosed with type 1 diabetes mellitus, about insulin safety. Which statement is appropriate?
options: Insulin is stable at room temperature for one month.
questions.correctAnswer: Insulin is stable at room temperature for one month.
questions.explanationText: Unopened insulin can typically be stored at room temperature for about one month after opening, depending on the type, while proper storage is essential for maintaining potency.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client taking lisinopril. Which finding is the priority to report immediately?
questions.correctAnswer: Swelling of the tongue
questions.explanationText: Swelling of the tongue may indicate angioedema, a life-threatening adverse effect of ACE inhibitors like lisinopril that requires immediate intervention due to risk of airway obstruction.

questions.questionText: A client with hypertension is prescribed atenolol. Which finding is an expected adverse effect of this medication?
questions.correctAnswer: Bradycardia
questions.explanationText: Atenolol is a beta-blocker that decreases heart rate and contractility. Bradycardia is a common and expected adverse effect and should be monitored.

questions.questionText: A client is prescribed hydrochlorothiazide for hypertension. What instruction should the nurse include in teaching?
questions.correctAnswer: Monitor for leg cramps
questions.explanationText: Hydrochlorothiazide is a thiazide diuretic that can cause electrolyte imbalances, particularly hypokalemia, which may result in muscle cramps.

questions.questionText: A nurse is preparing to administer a client’s antihypertensive medication. Which assessment finding should cause the nurse to hold the medication and further assess the client?
options: Urine output of 400 mL in the past 8 hours
Reports dizziness when ambulating to the bathroom
questions.correctAnswer: Reports dizziness when ambulating to the bathroom
questions.explanationText: Dizziness when ambulating suggests orthostatic hypotension, which can be worsened by antihypertensive medications and increases fall risk. Further assessment is needed before administration.

questions.questionText: A child with Addison’s disease is being cared for by the nurse. Which action should the nurse include in the plan of care?
options: Teach parents about cortisol replacement therapy
Place the child on a low sodium diet
questions.correctAnswer: Teach parents about cortisol replacement therapy
questions.explanationText: Addison’s disease involves adrenal insufficiency requiring lifelong corticosteroid (cortisol) replacement therapy to maintain hormone balance and prevent adrenal crisis.

questions.questionText: A nurse is assessing a client with Addison’s disease prior to surgery. Which finding should the nurse expect?
questions.correctAnswer: Hyperpigmentation
questions.explanationText: Addison’s disease causes increased ACTH production, which can lead to hyperpigmentation, especially in skin folds and pressure areas.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching a client about Cushing’s syndrome. Which statement by the client indicates understanding?
options: It is caused by too much cortisol made by my adrenal glands
It is caused by too little insulin in my body
It is caused by low thyroid hormone levels
It is caused by too much aldosterone only
questions.correctAnswer: It is caused by too much cortisol made by my adrenal glands
questions.explanationText: Cushing’s syndrome results from excess cortisol production, either from endogenous overproduction or exogenous corticosteroid use.

questions.questionText: A nurse is assessing a client newly diagnosed with peripheral neuropathy. Which manifestation should the nurse expect?
questions.correctAnswer: Intense burning sensations
questions.explanationText: Peripheral neuropathy commonly causes burning, tingling, or shooting pain due to nerve damage.

questions.questionText: A nurse is teaching an older adult client with diabetes mellitus about preventing long-term complications. Which instruction is most important?
questions.correctAnswer: Maintain stable blood glucose levels
questions.explanationText: Consistently controlled blood glucose levels are the most important factor in preventing long-term complications such as neuropathy, nephropathy, and retinopathy.

questions.questionText: A nurse is assessing a client for diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Which respiratory pattern should the nurse recognize as a compensatory response to metabolic acidosis?
options: Shallow respirations that improve after coughing
questions.correctAnswer: Deep, rapid respirations
questions.explanationText: Kussmaul respirations (deep, rapid breathing) are a compensatory mechanism to blow off carbon dioxide and reduce acidosis in DKA.

questions.questionText: Type 1 DM, feels shaky, hungry, and weak.  His blood sugar is 52 mg/dL
questions.correctAnswer: 4 starburst
questions.explanationText: This is a quick simple carb.

questions.questionText: A client's blood pressure reading is 189/110 mmHg during a community screening. They ask if they should be concerned. How should the student nurse respond?
options: Your blood pressure is very high. You need to see your healthcare provider today.
You have hypertension. You need to start making some lifestyle changes.
Please sit here quietly for a few minutes. I need to recheck your blood pressure.
I need you to remain seated. I am going to call 911/EMS.
questions.correctAnswer: Please sit here quietly for a few minutes. I need to recheck your blood pressure.
questions.explanationText: Blood pressure should be rechecked after the client has rested quietly before making recommendations or diagnosing hypertension.

questions.questionText: Which significant risk factors for hypertension does the student nurse identify from the client's health history? (Select all that apply.)
questions.explanationText: Alcohol use, smoking, and chronic stress are all significant modifiable risk factors for hypertension.

questions.questionText: The client's repeat blood pressure is 180/106 mmHg after sitting quietly. What is the most important recommendation for the student nurse to provide?
options: See their healthcare provider as soon as possible within the next week for a blood pressure recheck.
Limit salt intake and start a weight loss program.
Attend a stress reduction seminar offered in the community.
Learn about high fiber foods and add more fiber to the diet.
questions.correctAnswer: See their healthcare provider as soon as possible within the next week for a blood pressure recheck.
questions.explanationText: Hypertension diagnosis requires elevated blood pressure readings on multiple occasions. Follow-up with a healthcare provider is the priority.

questions.questionText: A healthcare provider diagnoses a client with stage 2 primary (essential) hypertension. What does this mean?
options: The client has hypertension that will need to be managed with medications.
This form of hypertension has been caused by another medical issue that needs to be addressed.
The client requires immediate hospitalization to treat their hypertension.
The client's blood pressure can be managed by lifestyle changes only; no medications are necessary.
questions.correctAnswer: The client has hypertension that will need to be managed with medications.
questions.explanationText: Stage 2 primary hypertension typically requires medication management in addition to lifestyle modifications.

questions.questionText: Which statements indicate the client understands teaching about chlorothiazide and atenolol? (Select all that apply.)
options: I should take chlorothiazide early in the morning and early evening.
I may initially experience hypotension when starting atenolol.
I should stop taking the medication once my blood pressure improves.
I should double my dose if I miss a dose.
correctAnswer: I should take chlorothiazide early in the morning and early evening.
I may initially experience hypotension when starting atenolol.
questions.explanationText: Diuretics should be taken earlier in the day to reduce nocturia. Atenolol may initially cause hypotension. Alcohol can worsen hypotension and interfere with treatment.

questions.questionText: Which action by the client's spouse demonstrates understanding of a 2 gram sodium diet?
options: Preparing a variety of fresh vegetables and avoiding processed foods.
Measuring out 2 teaspoons of salt each morning for cooking.
questions.correctAnswer: Preparing a variety of fresh vegetables and avoiding processed foods.
questions.explanationText: Processed foods are often high in sodium. A low sodium diet focuses on fresh foods and minimizing processed items.

questions.questionText: A client in hypertensive crisis is started on a nitroprusside infusion. What is the appropriate blood pressure management goal?
options: Decrease the blood pressure as quickly as possible.
Reduce the MAP by 25% in the next 24 hours.
Lower the MAP to 120 in the next hour.
Lower the systolic blood pressure to 120 mmHg in the next 24 hours.
questions.correctAnswer: Reduce the MAP by 25% in the next 24 hours.
questions.explanationText: Blood pressure should be lowered gradually in hypertensive emergencies to avoid organ hypoperfusion and ischemia.
